title: About
text:
Electoral Atlas · Spanish Second Republic · 1931–1936
  
    EN
    ES
    CA
  



  
  The team
  About the Project
  Who we are and what we are trying to understand




  
  The research
  Why map the Second Republic's elections?
  
    The Spanish Second Republic (1931–1939) held three parliamentary elections under universal
    suffrage — including the first election in Spain in which women could vote (1933). These
    elections took place in a highly polarised context and their results have been the subject
    of intense historical and political debate ever since.
  
  
    Yet comprehensive, systematic, municipal-level data for all three elections has
    never been assembled in a single, consistent, openly accessible dataset. This project
    fills that gap. By recovering and digitising results from provincial archives, the INE,
    and regional records, we aim to provide a resource for historians, political scientists,
    and the public alike.
  

  
    "The geography of the Republic's elections encodes the geography of the Civil War
    that followed. To understand one, you must understand the other."
    Project description, 2024
  

  
  Project history
  How we got here
  
    
      2021
      Project conceived as part of a broader research programme on the political economy of interwar Europe.
    
    
      2022
      First archival data collection phase: provincial capitals and large municipalities digitised from the Gaceta de Madrid.
    
    
      2023
      Funding secured. Data collection expanded to all 50 provinces. Research assistant hired.
    
    
      2024
      Beta version of the atlas launched. Codebook and methodology paper submitted for peer review.
    
    
      2025
      Public release of the full dataset and this interactive atlas.
    
  

  
  The team
  Who we are
  

    
      
      YN
      Your Name
      Principal Investigator
      
        Associate Professor in the Department of Political and Social Sciences at
        Universitat Pompeu Fabra. Research interests: electoral behaviour, historical
        political economy, Southern European politics.
      
      
        UPF profile
        Google Scholar
      
    

    
      RA
      Research Assistant
      Data Collection & Coding
      
        PhD candidate at Universitat Pompeu Fabra. Responsible for primary source
        collection, data coding, and quality control across all three election years.
      
      
        UPF profile

      Send a message
      Found an error?Have more data?
      
        This dataset is a work in progress. If you spot an error in the election results,
        know of additional sources, or have data that could improve coverage, we would love
        to hear from you. Use the form below — it opens your email client with the
        relevant fields pre-filled.

  Documentation
  Codebook
  Variable definitions, sources, and methodological notes



  Dataset documentation
  How to read the data
  
    The codebook describes every variable in the dataset: how each was collected,
    how it was coded, what the unit of analysis is, and any known limitations or
    caveats. It is the essential companion to the electoral atlas and to the
    underlying dataset.
  
  
    The full codebook is available as a PDF document below. It will be updated
    as the dataset evolves.
  

  
  
  
    📄
    
      Codebook PDF will appear here
      
        Place your file at pdfs/codebook.pdf and uncomment the iframe
      
    
  

  
  
    
      ↓   Download Codebook (PDF)

Vote share of left-wing parties

1931, 1933, 1936


right_[year]
Vote share of right-wing parties
0 – 1
1931, 1933, 1936


other_[year]
Vote share of other / regional parties
0 – 1
1931, 1933, 1936


turnout_[year]
Voter turnout (votes cast / registered voters)
0 – 1
1931, 1933, 1936

title: The Book
text:
Electoral Atlas · Spanish Second Republic · 1931–1936
  
    EN
    ES
    CA
  



  
  Publication
  The Book Project
  A monograph on the electoral geography of the Second Republic




  
  
    
    
      
      Electoral Geography
      Votes, Territory & the Fall of the Republic
      Your Name · 2025
    

    
    
      About the book
      Votes, Territory, and the Fall of the Spanish Republic

      
        
          Author
          Your Name
        
        
          Publisher
          TBA
        
        
          Expected
          2026
        
        
          Language
          English
        
      

      
        This book examines the territorial logic of political competition during the Spanish
        Second Republic. Drawing on a new municipal-level dataset of electoral results
        (1931–1936), it reconstructs how geography, class, religion, and local political
        organisations shaped voting patterns — and how those patterns translated into
        polarisation, democratic breakdown, and civil war.
      
      
        This atlas is the interactive companion to the book. All maps and figures can be
        explored here, and the underlying dataset is made freely available for replication
        and further research.
      

      ✉   Notify me on publication
      Contribute data
    
  

  
  Contents
  Chapter outline
  
    
      Intro
      
        Introduction: The Geography of Democracy
        Why electoral geography matters for understanding the Republic's trajectory.
      
    
    
      01
      
        Data and Methods
        Sources, coding decisions, coverage, and limitations of the municipal dataset.
      
    
    
      02
      
        April 1931: The Founding Election
        How the republican coalition swept Spain and where it was already weak.
      
    
    
      03
      
        November 1933: The Reversal
        Women's suffrage, fragmented left, and the rise of CEDA in the countryside.
      
    
    
      04
      
        February 1936: The Popular Front
        A narrow popular vote majority, its geographic distribution, and its contested aftermath.
      
    
    
      05
      
        Electoral Geography and the Civil War
        Does the 1936 electoral map predict the geography of the 1936–1939 conflict?
      
    
    
      Concl.
      
        Conclusion: Democracy, Territory, and Polarisation
        Comparative implications for interwar Europe and contemporary democracies.
      
    
  

  
  Related outputs
  
    
      Working paper
      "Municipal-Level Electoral Returns in the Spanish Second Republic: A New Dataset." 2024. Download
    
    
      Conference paper
      Presented at APSA 2024, ECPR 2024. Slides available on request.

title: Home
text:
Electoral Atlas · Spanish Second Republic · 1931–1936
  
    EN
    ES
    CA
  




  
  Spanish Second Republic · 1931–1939
  Electoral Atlas of theSecond Republic
  Municipal election results, 1931 · 1933 · 1936
  
  
    
      Elections
      3
    
    
      Municipalities
      ~9,000
    
    
      Years covered
      1931–36
    
  




  
    
    Universitat Pompeu Fabra
  
  
  
    
    Ministerio de Ciencia
  




  About this project
  Mapping democracy at themunicipal level
  
    This atlas presents the results of the three general elections held during the Spanish
    Second Republic (1931–1939) at the municipality level. By mapping electoral geography
    across all of Spain, it reveals the territorial patterns of political support — and the
    profound divisions that shaped the Republic's fate.
  
  
    The data covers over 9,000 municipalities across the three elections of April 1931,
    November 1933, and February 1936, allowing comparison of political shifts across
    regions, coalitions, and time.
  

  
    
      1931
      Republican–Socialist Coalition
      The founding election. A landslide for republican and socialist forces brought an end to the monarchy and established the Second Republic.
    
    
      1933
      Right-wing victory
      The first election with women's suffrage. A divided left allowed the CEDA and the Radicals to form a centre-right government.
    
    
      1936
      Popular Front
      The left reunited under the Popular Front narrowly defeated the National Front. The result was disputed and preceded the Civil War.
    
  

  Project details
  
    
      Data sources
      Historical electoral records, INE, regional archives
    
    
      Geography
      Historical municipal boundaries, 1930s
    
    
      Mapping tool
      Maplibre GL JS
    
    
      Built with
      R · Quarto · GitHub Pages
